text: The terms fundamentalist and fundamentalism were introduced in 1920 in the Northern Baptist Convention, an ecclesiastical body that comprised a segment of American Protestant denominations at war with modernism. Arguing that these terms were introduced as "flags of honour" by advocates of orthodoxy, the author maintains that they were soon castigated by liberals, undergoing what linguists call pejoration and lexical extension. By 1923, fundamentalist was also applied in the political journalism of the day to describe "the sticking to some creed, set of views or theory even when it is discredited" by the public at large. But the defining moment that shaped the currency of the terms in the north American and British minds of the common man occurred at the notoriously publicised Scopes "monkey trial" of 1925.
headline: Fully Human Written

By 1923, fundamentalist was also applied in the political journalism of the day to describe "the sticking to some creed, set of views or theory even when it is discredited" by the public at large. But the defining moment that shaped the currency of the terms in the north American and British minds of the common man occurred at the notoriously publicised Scopes "monkey trial" of 1925.
